title: Your data and privacy
description: Disconnect platforms, delete workspaces or your whole account, and understand how Vidflow handles your data.
headings: How Google and YouTube data is handled
body: You stay in control of the data Vidflow holds. This page covers disconnecting platforms, deleting content, deleting your account, and how Google and YouTube data is handled. You keep ownership of your content. Vidflow only receives the license it needs to operate the Service on your behalf. # How it works Disconnecting a platform or deleting a workspace acts directly on the underlying records, not a soft flag that quietly keeps data around. Disconnecting revokes the stored OAuth token immediately, so Vidflow loses the ability to post or read on your behalf on that platform from that moment on. Freezing a workspace halts every write path for it (no new episodes, clips, or posts), while permanent deletion removes its rows outright. Account-level deletion requests route through a manual, verified process rather than a self-service button, since it touches billing, connected platforms, and every workspace you own at once. # Disconnect a social platform Open Workspace Settings and go to the Connections tab. Under Connected Accounts , disconnect the account. Disconnecting immediately revokes Vidflow's stored access tokens for that platform and removes the associated profile data. # Delete a workspace Open Workspace Settings , then the Danger Zone tab. Two options: Freeze Workspace halts all operations immediately. The workspace is permanently deleted after 90 days unless you unfreeze it. Permanently Delete removes the workspace and all its content: episodes, clips, posts, schedules

title: Manage your subscription
description: Start a trial, switch plans, update your card, cancel, and request a refund.
headings: Update your card or view invoices
body: Everything billing-related lives on one page in the app, including current pricing for every plan. Reach for this page to start a plan, switch plans, update payment details, cancel, or request a refund. # How it works Vidflow does not run its own billing ledger, Stripe is the system of record for your card, invoices, and subscription state. When you start a trial or switch plans, Vidflow talks to Stripe to create or update the subscription, then a webhook syncs the result (plan, status, trial end date, brand space count) back into your account so the app always reflects what Stripe actually has on file. Because of that sync, changes made in the Stripe portal (canceling, updating a card) show up back in Vidflow automatically, no separate step needed. # Open the Billing page Click your avatar in the app. Choose Account settings . Click Manage plan & billing . The Billing & plan page shows your current plan and its status, your usage meters, and the plan catalog with pricing. If you are on a trial, a banner shows how many days remain. # Start a paid plan Every paid plan starts with a 7-day free trial. A card is required to start the trial. On the Billing page, pick a plan. Click Start 7-day trial . Complete checkout. Payment is handled by Stripe, and you can enter a promotion code during checkout. # Switch plans To upgrade or downgrade, click Switch to [plan name] on the plan you want. The change applies to your existing subscription immediately, and prorated charges or credits

title: Plans and features
description: What each Vidflow plan includes, from monthly allowances to feature access, and how free/trial branding funds the free tier.
headings: How the free tier funds itself
body: Vidflow has four plans: Free, Starter, Pro, and Agency. This page is the reference for what each plan includes. For current pricing, open the Billing page in the app. # How it works Every plan resolves to one record: a subscription row with a plan ID and status (trialing, active, past due, canceled, or none). The server checks that record before every metered action or gated feature, in real time, reading limits and feature flags from one shared plan table so pricing, limits, and gates never drift out of sync between what you see and what is actually enforced. # Plan limits Every allowance below resets monthly. Usage limits and monthly allowances explains how the meters work. Plan Brand spaces Working seats Episodes per month AI generations per month Clip generations per month Connected social accounts Free 1 1 1 20 3 1 Starter 1 1 2 150 30 3 Pro 1 2 8 600 120 10 Agency 1, expandable 10 8 2,500 500 Unlimited How to read the table: Brand spaces are workspaces. One brand space holds one brand's content, connected accounts, and settings. Everyone gets their first workspace, including on the Free plan. Agency owners can purchase additional brand spaces as an add-on at $49/month each. Working seats are admin and brand manager seats per brand space, including the owner. Client reviewer seats are free and unlimited on every plan. Episodes per month is a per-brand-space allowance. On Agency, each additional brand space adds its own episode allowance to your account total. AI generati

title: Account security
description: Set up two-factor authentication, manage recovery codes, and control where you are signed in.
body: Protect your Vidflow account with two-factor authentication (2FA) and review every device where you are signed in. Reach for this page when you are locking down an account with client data in it, onboarding a teammate with admin access, or you suspect a session you do not recognize. To open the Security page, click your avatar, choose Account settings , then click Two-factor & active sessions , or go directly to /app/security . # How 2FA works in Vidflow Two-factor authentication adds a second step to your sign-in: a 6-digit time-based code from an authenticator app, checked against a secret stored on your account. Recovery codes are single-use backups generated at enrollment and shown to you exactly once, Vidflow does not display them again after setup, so losing both your authenticator and your saved codes locks you out of the second factor entirely (support can help you back in by email). Important Important: 2FA applies when you sign in with your email and password. If you sign in with Google, that sign-in is protected by your Google account's own security settings instead. # Turn on two-factor authentication Open the Security page. Click Enable two-factor . Scan the QR code with your authenticator app. Enter the 6-digit code from the app to confirm. Save your recovery codes. Copy them or download the .txt file. Important Important: recovery codes are shown once. Each code works one time if you lose access to your authenticator app. Store them somewhere safe. After setup,

title: Usage limits and monthly allowances
description: How Vidflow's monthly allowances work, what counts against each one, and what to do at a cap.
headings: What counts as an AI generation
What counts as a clip generation
Free and trial branding is separate from these limits
body: Vidflow meters three things each month: episodes, AI generations, and clip generations. This page explains how the meters work, what counts against each one, and your options when you reach a cap. # How it works Every metered action checks your usage before it runs: Vidflow looks up your workspace owner's current plan and status, reads that plan's limit for the metric involved, and compares it to what you have used this calendar month. If you are under the limit, the action proceeds and the count goes up by one (or more, for batch actions); if you are at or over it, the action is blocked before anything is generated or charged, so a denied request never costs you a generation. # How allowances work Allowances reset at the start of each calendar month (UTC). Episodes are metered per brand space. Your account's monthly total is your plan's per-brand allowance multiplied by the brand spaces on your subscription. AI generations and clip generations are account-wide pools. All of your brand spaces draw from the same two pools. Your plan sets the numbers: Plan Episodes per month per brand space AI generations per month Clip generations per month Free 1 20 3 Starter 2 150 30 Pro 8 600 120 Agency 8 2,500 500 See Plans and features for the full plan matrix, including seats and connected account slots. # What counts as an episode Every long-form source you add counts as one episode, whichever way it arrives: a direct upload, a YouTube import, or an auto-import from Google Drive or Drop

title: Auto-import from Google Drive or Dropbox
description: Watch a cloud folder and turn every new file into a tagged, captioned clip in your Content Library.
body: Point Vidflow at a folder in Google Drive or Dropbox and every new file becomes a clip in your Content Library , transcribed, AI-titled, and captioned in your brand voice, with your tags already applied. # How it works Each source watches one cloud folder. New files are imported hourly, or on demand, as clips in the Content Library . Each imported clip is transcribed, AI-titled, and captioned. Your brand voice is always included in the AI instructions. Tags you choose are applied automatically, so tag-fed schedules pick the content up on their own. See Schedules and queues . Files are never deleted from the source folder, and files already imported are skipped on later polls. By default a source only imports files added after you create it. Use Import existing to backfill older files. # Create a source In the navigation rail, open Content Library , then Auto-Import . Click New Source . Choose Google Drive or Dropbox and connect your account. Vidflow asks for read-only access. Pick the folder to watch in the Select Folder to Watch dialog. New files in that folder now import automatically every hour. # Source settings Open a source's settings to control what happens to each imported clip: Auto-Apply Tags : tags added to every imported clip. Create tags on the Tags page first if you have none. See Tags . Require Approval : imported clips are marked "Needs Approval" and will not publish until manually approved. See Approval status in your library . Platform-Specific Captions : pi

title: Bulk upload and CSV import
description: Add many files at once with the Bulk Upload wizard, or queue weeks of pre-written posts from a CSV.
body: The Bulk Upload page does two jobs. A drag-and-drop wizard turns a batch of files into library items with AI titles and captions, and a CSV importer creates posts you have already written. # How it works The two tools on this page solve different problems and don't overlap. The wizard is for raw media you haven't written copy for yet, it uploads each file and has the AI draft a title, caption, and tags for it, optionally reading the episode you link it to for context. The CSV importer is the opposite: it's for posts whose copy you've already written elsewhere, it just gets that copy scheduled without touching the AI at all. # The Bulk Upload wizard Note Note: The wizard is included on the Pro and Agency plans. The CSV importer further down the page works on any plan. See Plans and features . The wizard runs in three steps: Drop Files , Review , and Process . Click Make Content , then Bulk Upload . Drag your files in, or click to browse. Video, audio, images, plain text, markdown, and CSV files are supported. Set Link to source episode (optional but recommended) . Linking to an episode gives the AI context to write better titles and captions. If you stage clips without one, Vidflow shows a Missing Episode Context warning before it processes them. Click Review Files , check the list, then click Process All . Vidflow uploads the files, writes an AI title, caption, and tags for each one, and adds everything to your Content Library . When it finishes, use View in Library to see th

title: Episodes vs. library content
description: How long-form source episodes relate to the posts, clips, and images you publish.
headings: Link content to its source episode
body: Vidflow separates what you make content from and what you publish. Episodes are your long-form sources. Library content is the individual pieces that go out to your channels. # How it works An episode never publishes on its own, it's raw material. Every clip, post, image, or article that does publish is generated by reading an episode's transcript (or, for library content with no linked episode, standing alone with less context). Keeping this split means the same episode can feed dozens of pieces of content over time without you re-uploading or re-explaining it each time. # Episodes: your source material An episode is a long-form source: a video or audio file you upload, or a video imported from YouTube. The episode carries the transcript, show notes, and metadata that the AI works from when it writes clips, captions, and posts. You create episodes by: Uploading a file. See Upload a video or audio file . Importing from YouTube, either from a connected channel or by pasting any YouTube links. See Import from YouTube . Handing the Clip Generator a file or YouTube link. It saves the video to your library as a new episode, then clips it. See Generate clips from a long video . Adding your very first episode on the onboarding first-episode screen, which also kicks off a starter content pack automatically. See Your first content pack . Each episode counts toward your plan's monthly episode allowance. See Usage limits and monthly allowances . # Library content: what you publish The C

title: Import from YouTube
description: Import videos from your connected YouTube channel, or paste any YouTube links directly, to bring in episodes with metadata and transcripts.
body: Bring videos into Vidflow as episodes straight from YouTube, either by browsing your own connected channel or by pasting links to any videos you want. Either way, each import pulls the video's metadata and transcript only. The video file itself is never downloaded. # How it works Both import modes create one episode per video and start pulling transcripts in the background, so you can keep working while they process. Neither mode downloads the actual video file, only its metadata and transcript, so a YouTube-imported episode has no attached media. That single fact drives one real limitation: native clip generation needs an uploaded video file to cut from, and a YouTube import never provides one. If you want clips from a video you import, upload the file separately for that episode. # Import from your connected channel Use this mode when the videos you want are already on your own YouTube channel. On the Episodes page, click Import Episodes . You can also click Make Content , then Import Episodes , from anywhere in the app. In the import dialog, make sure Connect channel is selected at the top. Choose the Videos or Shorts tab. The dialog lists your channel's uploads. Click Load more to page further back. Select the videos you want. You can import up to 50 videos at a time. Videos already in your workspace show an Imported badge and cannot be selected again. Click Import into Library . This mode requires a connected YouTube channel. Connect it in Brand Settings > Connected Acco

title: Transcripts
description: How Vidflow transcribes your episodes, and how to retry, re-pull, or download a transcript.
body: Every episode gets a transcript, and much of Vidflow works from it: clips, captions, and AI writing all read the transcript. Transcription starts automatically after an upload or import. # How it works An uploaded video or audio file is transcribed with AI speech-to-text, word by word, against the actual audio. A YouTube-imported episode skips that step entirely and pulls the video's existing YouTube captions instead, which is faster but only as accurate as YouTube's own captions were. Either way, the result is the same shape of transcript, and everything downstream, clip selection, captions, AI writing, reads it the same regardless of which path produced it. # How transcripts are generated Uploaded files are transcribed with AI speech-to-text. Episodes imported from YouTube pull the video's transcript instead. Vidflow transcribes in English today. Note Note: Transcribing an upload counts as one AI generation against your plan's monthly allowance. See Usage limits and monthly allowances . # Track transcription status A transcript moves through pending, processing, and completed states, or failed if something went wrong. The episode shows a status badge. While a transcription runs, the notifications bell in the header lists it as a Transcription job and links to your episodes. # Start or retry transcription If an episode shows "Transcript not yet generated", open it and click Transcribe Now . After a failed attempt, the same button retries. It is disabled while a transcription

title: Upload a video or audio file
description: Add a long-form video or audio file to Vidflow and let transcription and AI metadata run automatically.
headings: Where you can start an upload
Start from a YouTube URL instead
body: Upload a long-form video or audio file and Vidflow saves it as an episode, starts the transcript, and drafts a title, description, and tags in the background. # How it works Your file uploads in chunks so large files transfer reliably, then Vidflow assembles it, saves an episode record immediately, and kicks off transcription and AI metadata generation in the background. This is the only way to get an episode with an actual video file attached, which matters later: native clip generation needs that file, and a YouTube import alone never provides it. # Where you can start an upload Click Make Content at the top of the app, then Upload Episode . Click Upload Episode on the Episodes page. If the Episodes view of your Content Library is empty, use its Upload Video button. In the Clip Generator, the Upload file option uploads your file to the library as a new episode, then clips it. See Generate clips from a long video . # Upload a file Click Make Content , then Upload Episode . Drag your file into the dialog, or click Browse files . Vidflow accepts video and audio files: MP4, MOV, MP3, WAV, and M4A. Keep the tab open while the upload runs. Large files upload in chunks automatically, and the dialog shows progress as each part completes. Note Note: There is no fixed file size cap. Files over 1GB show a warning that the upload may take several minutes, keep the tab open until it finishes. When the upload finishes, Vidflow saves the episode as a draft immediately. Transcription and A

title: AI images and thumbnails
description: Generate images for posts in Image Studio or standalone in the Content Library, enhance clip thumbnails, build carousels, and make light image edits.
headings: Image Presets in Brand Settings (Pro and Agency)
body: Vidflow generates images where you already work: an Image Studio inside the post editor, a standalone generator in the Content Library, AI thumbnails in the clip editor, and a light image editor for crops and filters. Every image generation counts toward your plan's monthly AI allowance. See Usage limits and monthly allowances . # How it works Your prompt supplies the subject, and your workspace's saved image style, set in Brand Settings or during the onboarding wizard, supplies the look: color palette, composition, mood, whatever you described when you set it up. When a style is set, it is applied before any other style choice the AI would otherwise make on its own, so the same prompt produces a consistent look across every image you generate instead of a different style each time. With AI Quality Pass turned on, a vision model checks each result against your brand rubric and regenerates automatically until it passes, up to three attempts, before handing you the result. # Image Studio Image Studio generates and refines images for a text or image post. Open a text or image post from your Content Library in the edit view. Click Image Studio . The studio opens beside the post; Close Studio hides it again. Describe the image in the prompt box, or click Magic prompt to draft a prompt from the post's title and caption. Pick an Aspect Ratio : 3:4, 4:5, 1:1, 9:16, 16:9, or 1.91:1. Each option notes the placements it suits, such as feed, story, or link preview. Optional: pick a Socia

title: Blog Post Generator
description: Generate long-form articles from an episode, script, or prompt, then edit, copy, download, or save them to your library.
body: The Blog Post Generator turns an episode, script, or prompt into a long-form HTML article styled for LinkedIn Articles, Substack, or general publishing. You edit the draft directly in the preview, then copy it, download it, or save it to your Content Library. Find it as Blog Post under Magic Tools in the left rail. Note: Article generation is included on the Pro and Agency plans. On other plans the page opens but Generate Article is locked. See Plans and features . # How it works The AI writes from your chosen source (an episode transcript, a saved script, or a plain prompt) and shapes it using the platform style, tone, and length you picked. Your workspace's brand voice settings, brand sheet, target audience, and voice profile, are folded into the same prompt, so the platform and tone controls set the article's structure while your brand voice settings set how it actually sounds. # Generate an article Pick a source: Episode , Script , or Prompt . Pick a platform style: LinkedIn Article (professional, high-signal, comment-friendly), Substack (newsletter-style, personal, opinionated), or General (the default). Pick a tone: Professional , Conversational (the default), Thought Leadership , or Educational . Pick a length: Short (around 600 words), Medium (around 1,200, the default), or Long (around 2,000). Add anything specific under Additional Instructions . Click Generate Article . With an episode as the source, the article is written from its transcript. The page confirms when

title: Idea Generator and Idea Library
description: Generate content ideas in your brand voice, save the keepers, and turn the best ones into scripts.
body: The Idea Generator brainstorms content ideas in your brand voice. The Idea Library keeps the ones you save, tracks which you have used, and feeds them straight into the Script Generator. Both live under Magic Tools in the left rail: Idea Generator , with Idea Library beneath it. # How it works With no topic entered, the AI brainstorms from your workspace's brand voice settings and past content, so the batch reflects what you actually talk about and who you talk to, not a generic list of content ideas. A topic narrows that same brainstorm to one subject. Either way, your target audience and brand sheet shape the angle each idea takes, and the same context carries forward automatically when you turn an idea into a script. # Generate ideas Open Idea Generator . Pick a Number of ideas : 3, 6, or 10 (6 is the default). Add a Topic or theme (optional) , or leave it blank to generate ideas based on your brand voice and past content. Click Generate Ideas . Two ways to steer the batch: Watch trending suggests angles to explore. Suggestions appear as pills; click one to add it to your topic. If nothing comes back, run it again. Start from a top performer appears once your analytics show top episodes. Pick an episode to riff on and the generator seeds itself from that episode's transcript. Each generated idea appears as a card. Click Save to keep it in your Idea Library, or Outline to expand a quick outline before you decide. Note: If your brand voice is not set up, the page shows a ban

title: Magic Text Posts
description: Turn an episode, script, or prompt into a batch of text posts in your brand voice, saved to your library, a queue, or the calendar.
body: Magic Text Posts turns one source, an episode transcript, a saved script, or a plain prompt, into a batch of text posts written in your brand voice. You review the batch, then send everything to your Content Library, a queue, or the calendar in one step. Find it under Magic Tools in the left rail. # How it works Every generation reads your workspace's brand voice settings, your brand sheet, target audience, and voice profile if you have built one from a YouTube channel, and folds them straight into the prompt the AI writes from. That is the difference between a generic post and one that sounds like your brand: the same source material and the same post type will read differently in two workspaces with different brand voice settings. Post type (Quote, Insight, Tip, and so on) shapes the structure, source material supplies the substance, and your brand voice settings supply the actual voice. # Before you start Set up your brand voice. Until you do, the page shows a banner reading "Your brand voice isn't set up yet" and the AI writes in a generic voice. See Set up your brand voice . To generate from an episode, that episode needs a transcript. Episodes that have one show a green transcript badge in the source picker. See Transcripts . Each generation counts toward your plan's monthly AI allowance. See Usage limits and monthly allowances . # Generate a batch Open Magic Text Posts and pick a content source: From Episode / Transcript , From Script , or Free Prompt / Topic . Leave P

title: Script Generator
description: Draft full video and podcast scripts in your voice, refine any passage with AI, and save them to your script library.
body: The Script Generator writes a complete script for a YouTube video, podcast episode, or short-form video in your brand voice, sized to the runtime you want. You edit it as one document, refine any passage with AI, and save it to your script library. Find it under Magic Tools in the left rail. Script Library , beneath it, lists everything you have saved. # How it works The AI writes to your target runtime by first converting minutes into a target word count at the format's real speaking pace, then drafting a script that hits that length. Your brand voice settings, brand sheet, target audience, and voice profile, shape the wording throughout, the same way they shape every other AI writer in Vidflow. Rewrite, Expand, and Shorten on a selected passage reuse that same brand context, so an AI-edited passage should still sound like the rest of the script, not like a different writer took over. # Generate a script Open Script Generator and click New Script . In Generation settings , pick a format: YouTube Video , Podcast Episode , or Short-Form . Set Target Video Length in minutes, from 1 to 180 (10 is the default). The panel estimates the spoken word count at the format's speaking pace: 145 words per minute for YouTube, 150 for podcast, 160 for short-form. Describe the topic in What's this script about? Click Generate Script . Note: Scripts read like you only after your brand voice is set up; until then the page shows a banner and the AI writes in a generic voice. See Set up your bra

title: Analytics overview
description: What the Analytics page tracks, when data appears, and what each card shows.
body: The Analytics page tracks your content output and audience growth for the current workspace. This article covers when data appears and what each card measures. # How it works The page combines three separate data feeds into one view: your own publishing and content-production history (always current), daily follower snapshots for connected accounts, and a roughly-6-hour poll of per-post engagement metrics from each supported platform. The first two power the cards on this page; the third powers the Post Performance table, covered in its own article since it has its own supported-platform list and refresh timing. See Post performance . # When data appears Analytics fill in as you connect social accounts and publish posts. A new workspace shows a "We're still collecting your data" card with a Connect accounts button until there is something to chart. Two things feed the page: Publishing through Vidflow. Post now, scheduled posts, and posts published by flows all count. Posts you publish directly on a platform, outside Vidflow, do not appear in the publishing cards. Connected accounts. Vidflow records follower counts for your connected accounts daily, which powers the Audience card. # Pick a period The period selector offers Last 7 days , Last 30 days , and Last 90 days . It defaults to 30. The period scopes the Audience change figures, the Posts card, and the Publishing Activity chart. The Total Episodes , Total Clips , and Published Posts cards are workspace totals and do not

title: Branded client reports
description: Generate a performance report carrying your logo and brand color, then save it as a PDF from the print dialog.
body: Generate a client-ready performance report styled with your own branding, then save it as a PDF. The report is built from the same data as the Analytics page. # How it works Clicking Branded report builds a standalone HTML page server-side, using the same numbers the Analytics page shows for the period you have selected, and stamps it with your workspace's logo and brand color instead of Vidflow's. The page opens in a new tab and triggers your browser's print dialog automatically, so "export" is really "print to PDF" using the browser you already have open. Nothing is uploaded or emailed; the file only exists once you save it from the print dialog. # What the report includes The report carries your workspace logo (or your workspace initial if no logo is set), your workspace name, and your brand color. The header reads "Performance Report" with the covered period and the date it was generated. Sections: Overview : posts published, scheduled, awaiting approval, episodes, clips created, and total content. Audience : followers and change per platform. Published by platform : how published posts split across platforms. Top episodes : your top episodes ranked by clips generated. Content pipeline : draft, scheduled, and published counts. The footer shows your workspace name and a small "Prepared with Vidflow" line. Note Note: The report does not include the per-post view, like, comment, and share numbers from Post Performance . It covers publishing volume, audience, and pipeline; fo

title: Post performance
description: See views, likes, comments, and shares for individual posts, which platforms are supported, and how to read the table.
body: The Post Performance table on the Analytics page shows how your individual posts are doing on the platforms where Vidflow can read that data back, not just how much you published. # How it works After a post publishes through Vidflow, a background process checks in with each supported platform roughly every 6 hours and pulls its current view, like, comment, and share counts. Each pull overwrites that day's numbers rather than piling up duplicate rows, so what you see is always each post's latest count for that day, not a raw event log. Metrics are collected only for posts Vidflow actually published; anything you posted directly on a platform, outside Vidflow, has no way to be matched back to a library item. # What's collected Views : total views or impressions, where the platform exposes that number. Likes : likes or reactions. Comments : comment count. Shares : shares, retweets and quotes on X, or reposts, where the platform exposes that number. Not every platform reports every metric. For example, YouTube's public API does not expose a share count, and Facebook post-level view counts require a separate permission Vidflow does not request yet, so those individual numbers read as 0 rather than being estimated. # Which platforms are supported Supported today : X, YouTube, Instagram, Facebook. Not yet supported : TikTok and LinkedIn. Vidflow can publish to both, but neither platform's API currently gives this app a reliable way to read metrics back for a specific post, so their

title: Approval status in your library
description: How approval badges work across the Content Library and what they mean for scheduled publishing.
body: Every content item can carry an approval state, and that state decides whether scheduled publishing will touch it. This article covers the badges, how content enters review, and how approval interacts with publishing. # How it works Approval status lives on the content item itself, separate from its publish status, and separate from any specific approval link. A schedule with Require Approval turned on checks that state at dispatch time and holds back anything that isn't Approved; a schedule without it ignores approval state entirely and publishes on schedule regardless. That's also why Post Now can send unreviewed content, it has no schedule attached to check against, so it never looks at approval state at all. # The badges Needs Approval : the item is waiting for review. Inside the item view the badge reads Pending Approval . Approved : the item has been cleared and publishes normally. Rejected : the item has been marked as not cleared. Rejected items are held back from scheduled publishing the same way pending items are. You see these badges in the item view in your Content Library and on the client review page. In the library toolbar, the approval filter switches between All Approval States , Needs Approval , and Approved , and the sort menu includes Unapproved First . Approval state changes when your client acts on the review page: approving an item marks it Approved , and undoing that returns it to Needs Approval . # Send an item into review Open the item in your Conten

title: Client approvals overview
description: Send your client one link to review and approve content before it publishes. No client account needed.
body: Approvals let you put finished content in front of a client from a single shareable link. The client reviews on any device, with no Vidflow account and no login. # Why approval links If you run content for clients, the review step is usually the slowest part of the pipeline: screenshots pasted into email threads, "any word on last week's posts?" follow-ups, and change requests scattered across three channels. An approval link replaces that. You pick the content, Vidflow generates one URL, and everything the client needs lives behind it. The page carries your logo, your workspace name, and your brand color. Vidflow's branding does not appear, so to your client it reads as your review portal. # How it works Create a link from the Approvals page. Give it a title, pick the content, and set an optional expiry between 1 and 90 days. Send the URL to your client through whatever channel you already use. The client opens it in a browser and approves items or leaves comments. There is no reject button; change requests come back as comments. Every approval and comment saves the moment it happens. There is no submit step for the client to forget. You check progress from the Approvals page. Vidflow does not send you a notification when a client approves or comments, so build the check into your routine. Once an item is approved, it is cleared to publish. Scheduled posts that are still waiting on approval are held back, then go out after approval. # Where approvals plug in Schedules : ever

title: What your client sees
description: The review page from your client's side, including approving, undoing, and commenting, and how you see the results.
body: When your client opens an approval link, they land on a review page branded as yours. This article walks through that page and what happens with everything they do on it. # How it works The review page reads directly from the same approval batch and approval status your agency side sees, there's no separate client-facing copy of the data. Every click, an approval, an undo, a comment, writes straight to that record the instant it happens, which is why there's no submit button and no way for a client to lose unsaved work by closing the tab early. # No login, your brand The link opens in any browser. Your client does not create an account, set a password, or sign in to anything. The page header shows your workspace logo, your workspace name, and the link title. If you have not uploaded a logo, a check mark in your brand color stands in. The progress bar picks up your brand color too. Vidflow's branding does not appear; to your client, this is your review portal. Set your logo and brand color in your workspace's brand settings. See Set up your brand voice . # What your client can do Each content item appears as a card with its media (video player or image), its caption, a status badge, and a comments panel. Approve : the green Approve button marks the item approved. The badge updates immediately. Undo an approval : an approved item's button turns into Approved, click to undo . Clicking it returns the item to Needs Approval . Approve All : while items are still open, an "Approve a

title: Create and share an approval link
description: Bundle content into a review link, set an optional expiry, and manage the link after you send it.
body: Create a link that bundles content for a client to review, then copy it and send it. This article covers creating, sharing, closing, and reopening links. # How it works Creating a link snapshots the content you selected into a batch and generates a random share token, the URL your client opens is just that token. The link carries no login of its own; opening it is enough to see and act on everything in the batch. Approving an item on the review page updates that item's approval status immediately and directly, so the moment your client clicks Approve, it's reflected in your Content Library and to any schedule checking that item's approval state, with no separate sync step. # Before you start Approvals are available on paid plans, Starter and up. On the Free plan the Create Approval Link button is locked and prompts you to upgrade. See Plans and features . The content you want reviewed needs to be in your Content Library first. # Create the link In the app rail, open Approvals (in the Publish section). Click Create Approval Link . If this is your first link, the page shows a Create First Link button instead. Enter a Link Title , for example "Week 12 Content Review". The client sees this title when they open the link. Optionally set Expires In (days) to a number from 1 to 90. Leave it blank and the link never expires. Under Select Content , open the picker and select the items the client should review. Click Create Link . # Share it The dialog switches to Approval Link Created

title: Caption styles
description: The full library of animated caption styles for clips, what each one looks like, and when to pick it.
body: Every clip gets animated, word-timed captions burned into the video. Vidflow ships seven caption styles, from a plain default to bold pop-up text to a boxed style built for busy footage. Pick one when you generate a clip, or switch styles later and re-render. # How it works Captions are generated from the clip's word-level transcript, so every word is timed to when it is actually spoken, not just placed on screen for a fixed duration. Each style controls the font, color, position, and animation: some pop each word in one at a time, some fade in whole phrases, one fills karaoke-style as the words are spoken, and one draws a solid background box behind the text so it stands out against anything happening in the footage. Certain words are automatically picked out for emphasis (a punch-word highlight in the accent color), which most styles use to draw the eye to the key phrase in a line. # The styles Style Look Best for Standard The house default: clean white-gray uppercase text, centered, no outline, a soft shadow. Punch words highlight in white. A neutral default that works on most footage without calling attention to itself. Box Background Same clean uppercase text as Standard, but each line sits inside its own solid black rounded box. Light or busy footage where plain white text does not pop on its own. Bold Center Pop Large bold text, one word at a time, punching in and scaling up as it's spoken. Punch words flash yellow. High-energy moments where you want the captions thems

title: Clips troubleshooting
description: Fix the most common problems with generating and editing clips.
headings: "OpusClip isn't connected for this workspace yet"
"Native clips need the actual video file"
You have reached your monthly clip allowance
Captions are hard to read against the footage
A rendered clip has a watermark and end card
body: Common problems when generating or editing clips, and what to do about each one. # "OpusClip isn't connected for this workspace yet" This appears in the Clip Generator when you select the OpusClip engine but no OpusClip API key is connected for the current workspace. The Generate Clips button is disabled until you fix this. Fix it one of two ways: Go to Workspace Settings > Connections and connect this workspace's OpusClip API key. See Using OpusClip with Vidflow . Or switch to the Native engine, which needs no connector. # "Native clips need the actual video file" This appears when you try to run the Native engine on an episode that was added as a link (for example, a YouTube URL) with no uploaded media. Native cannot work from a link alone. Fix it one of two ways: Upload the video file to that episode, then run Native again. Or switch to the OpusClip engine, which can fetch link-only sources itself. # Generation seems stuck After you click Generate Clips , the status card reads "Generating your clips." with "This usually takes a few minutes." Most runs finish within that window. Note Note: If clips have not appeared in your Content Library after roughly 30 minutes, re-run the generation, or contact support if it happens again. Do not assume a failed run will always show an error message on screen. Before re-running, check the Clips tab in your Content Library directly. Clips are added there automatically as soon as they finish rendering, and this is more reliable than watch

title: Edit clips: captions, reframing, thumbnails
description: Open a generated clip to rewrite its captions, adjust reframing, swap the video, or pick a new thumbnail.
body: Once a clip lands in your Content Library, open it to rewrite the title and captions in your brand voice, adjust how it is reframed and captioned, pick a new thumbnail, or swap the underlying video. # How it works Your edits in the clip detail dialog, reframing, caption style, on-screen hook text, are saved as instructions rather than applied instantly. Clicking Render Edited Clip is what actually burns those instructions into a fresh MP4, running the same rendering module clips go through when they are first generated. The original clip file stays untouched until you render, so you can try a different crop or caption style and re-render as many times as you want without losing your starting point. # Open a clip From the Content Library, open a clip item to reach the clip detail dialog. It has three tabs: Details , Reframe , and Captions . Details : review the clip, approve it, share it, and render edited exports. Reframe : adjust the crop used to reframe the clip, or turn on a gentle pan/zoom. Captions : edit caption timing. # Adjust the reframing On the Reframe tab: Crop : drag the Left Offset , Top Offset , Width , and Height sliders to move and resize the crop window against a preview of the clip. Pan/Zoom : turn on Enable gentle pan/zoom and set a Duration to add a slow pan across the frame instead of a static crop. This is saved as metadata and applied the next time the clip is rendered or exported. Click Save Reframing to keep your changes. # AI Clip Writing The AI Cli

title: Generate clips from a long video
description: Turn an episode, an uploaded file, or a YouTube link into short clips with the Clip Generator.
body: Use the Clip Generator to turn a long-form video into a batch of short clips. Pick a source, choose an engine, set your options, and Vidflow finds the moments and renders the clips for you. # How it works Vidflow ships two clip engines. Native, the default, is built in house: it transcribes your video word by word, has an AI read that transcript to score and pick the strongest moments, then reframes, captions, and renders each one on Vidflow's own infrastructure, no third-party account needed. OpusClip is an optional engine you connect with your own OpusClip API key; it fetches and clips the source video on OpusClip's side, which is why it can work from a bare YouTube link where Native cannot. # Before you start You need a source video: an existing episode with a video file, an uploaded video or audio file, or a YouTube link. You also need at least one clip engine available. Vidflow ships two: Native , Vidflow's in-house engine. No setup, works out of the box, and needs no API key. OpusClip , a third-party engine that requires your own OpusClip API key connected for the workspace. See The Native clip engine and Using OpusClip with Vidflow for what each one offers. # Open the Clip Generator From the app rail, click Clip Generator . Or, from an episode's detail page, click Generate Clips to open the Clip Generator with that episode preselected. Or, from a Content Library card's menu, choose Generate Clips . # Pick a source Under Source , choose one of three tabs: Existing episo

title: The Native clip engine
description: Generate clips with Vidflow's built-in engine, no third-party account required.
body: Native is Vidflow's in-house clip engine and the default choice in the Clip Generator. It selects moments from the transcript, reframes the video around the speaker, burns in captions, and renders the clips on Vidflow's own infrastructure. There is no third-party key to connect and no setup required. Beta note Beta note: The Native engine is in beta. It is the default engine in the Clip Generator. # How it works Native runs in two stages. First, a selection pass transcribes your video word by word, then has an AI read that transcript and score candidate moments for hook strength and virality, the same kind of scoring an editor would use to judge whether a moment is worth cutting into a short. It uses the full range of the scale rather than rating everything as a hit, so weaker moments genuinely score lower and get passed over. Second, each selected moment goes through an automatic render job. A face-detection pass finds the speaker in every scene so the crop follows them instead of the middle of the frame, filling the vertical frame around the speaker by default (called "fill" reframing) rather than shrinking the whole picture down with black bars. Captions are then generated and burned in using your chosen style, timed word by word to match the transcript, and a thumbnail is pulled from about a third of the way into the clip. Finished clips appear as clip items in your Content Library, tagged with a Native source, and count toward your workspace's clip generation allowance.

title: Using OpusClip with Vidflow
description: Connect your own OpusClip API key to generate clips with brand templates, ClipAnything, and more.
body: OpusClip is a third-party clip engine you connect to Vidflow with your own API key. Because it fetches source video itself, it works for YouTube-link episodes as well as uploaded files. It is entirely optional, bring your own key: Vidflow's Native engine needs no key and is the default in the Clip Generator. Each workspace connects its own OpusClip account. Your clips, credits, and brand templates stay on your OpusClip account, not a shared Vidflow key. # How it works Vidflow sends your source video and options to OpusClip's API. OpusClip does its own moment selection and rendering on its servers, using whichever brand template and clip mode you chose, then hands the finished clips back through a webhook. Vidflow copies each finished clip into its own storage as soon as it arrives, so it stays reachable in your library even after OpusClip's own links expire. Because OpusClip fetches and downloads the source itself, it can work from a bare YouTube link, unlike Native, which needs an uploaded file. # Connect your OpusClip API key Go to Workspace Settings , then the Connections tab. Find the OpusClip card. If it is not connected, you will see Connect required . Click Connect OpusClip account . Paste your OpusClip API key. You can find it in the OpusClip dashboard under Settings > API . Optionally add an Organization ID (the x-opus-org-id header), needed only for multi-org OpusClip accounts. Click Test & Save . Vidflow validates the key with a zero-credit call before saving it. O

title: Let clients connect their own accounts
description: Mint a shareable link so a client or talent can connect their own social accounts to your workspace without signing in to Vidflow.
body: A Vidflow Connect link lets someone who owns the social accounts, a client, a talent, a partner brand, connect their own platforms to your workspace without ever signing in to Vidflow itself. You send the link, they sign in with each platform on their end, and the accounts show up ready to publish in your workspace. Note Note: Connect links are included on every paid plan, Starter and up. On the Free plan, New Connect Link is locked and prompts you to upgrade. See Plans and features . # How it works A connect link is a token tied to your workspace, with no password or account behind it, anyone who has the link can open it. When your client signs in through one of the real OAuth buttons on that page, the resulting connection is stored on your workspace exactly as if you had connected it yourself from Workspace Settings, and it can publish immediately. The "add manually" option instead just records a name and handle with no platform authorization at all, so it can't publish until someone completes the real sign-in later, from the link or from inside your workspace directly. # Create a link Open Brand Settings . Click the Accounts tab. Click the Vidflow Connect sub-tab. Type a label for the link, for example the client or brand name. Click New Connect Link . Copy the link from the table, or click the preview icon to open it in a new tab. By default a connect link never expires. Set an expiry in days when you create it if you want the link to stop working after a set window. # Wh

title: Connect Instagram and Facebook
description: Link Instagram and Facebook together through one Meta connection and see what each platform can publish.
body: Instagram and Facebook connect through a single Meta sign-in. One connection can link both platforms at once, since Vidflow discovers your Facebook Page and any Instagram professional account attached to it during the same flow. # How it works Publishing runs through Meta's Graph API using your Facebook Page as the anchor. Instagram videos post as Reels through a two-step process: Vidflow creates a media container from your video, waits for Meta to finish processing it, then publishes the container. Facebook picks the right endpoint automatically based on what you're posting, video goes to the Page's video endpoint with a title and description, image goes to photos, text-only goes to the feed. Both platforms rely on the same stored Meta token, so a problem with one (like an expired token) affects both at once. # What you need before connecting A Facebook Page . Vidflow publishes to Facebook through a Page, not a personal profile. For Instagram, a professional (business) Instagram account linked to that Facebook Page . A personal Instagram account with no Page connection will not appear. If your Instagram is not yet set up as a professional account linked to a Page, do that in Meta's own settings first, then connect in Vidflow. # Connect steps Open Workspace Settings . Click the Connections tab. Click Facebook & Instagram . Sign in on Meta's page and approve access, including access to your Pages. You are returned to Vidflow. Vidflow links your Facebook Page automatically, and

title: Connect LinkedIn
description: Link LinkedIn to Vidflow to publish posts, and know when to expect a reconnect.
body: Connect LinkedIn to publish posts to your LinkedIn profile from Vidflow. # How it works Vidflow posts through LinkedIn's UGC Posts API using the person URN captured when you connected. A video post attaches your media as a ready-to-view asset alongside your text; a post with no video, or with an image, publishes as text only, since there's no verified image-attachment path today. LinkedIn's stored token has no background refresh, so publishing checks the token's expiry before every attempt and fails fast with a clear reconnect message rather than surfacing LinkedIn's own generic API error. # Connect steps Open Workspace Settings . Click the Connections tab. Click LinkedIn . Sign in on LinkedIn's page and approve access. You are returned to Vidflow with LinkedIn listed as connected. # What Vidflow can publish Text and video posts to your connected LinkedIn profile, from Post now on library content and from scheduled dispatch on both content items and legacy queue posts. # Limits enforced Title: 200 characters. # Token lifetime and reconnect LinkedIn tokens last roughly 60 days. Expect a periodic reconnect prompt as that window closes. When LinkedIn expires the authorization, Vidflow pauses publishing to that account and emails you explaining what happened. Reconnecting from the Connections tab picks back up anything waiting on your schedule. # Get better results Expect the ~60-day reconnect and plan around it , especially for a client workspace you don't check daily. Put a rec

title: Connect your social accounts
description: Where to connect social accounts in Vidflow, what statuses mean, and how reconnecting and disconnecting work.
body: Connect your social accounts once per workspace so Vidflow can publish and schedule content on your behalf. This article covers where connections live and what happens as accounts age, expire, or get disconnected. For steps on a specific platform, use the per-platform articles linked below. # How it works Connecting an account runs you through that platform's real login page, where you approve Vidflow's access, and hands back an access token Vidflow stores for your workspace. Every publish or schedule attempt to that platform uses the stored token rather than asking you to sign in again. Platforms that support it (X, TikTok, YouTube) have their tokens refreshed automatically in the background before they expire; platforms that don't (Meta, LinkedIn) rely on a longer-lived token that eventually needs a manual reconnect. Either way, Vidflow only finds out a token is truly dead when a publish attempt or a background check fails against the platform's own API. # Where to connect Open Workspace Settings . Click the Connections tab. Choose a platform to connect: YouTube, Instagram & Facebook, TikTok, LinkedIn, or X (Twitter). Clicking a platform starts that platform's real sign-in flow. You will leave Vidflow briefly, approve access on the platform's own page, and land back in Vidflow once the connection completes. Every workspace is its own brand. Accounts you connect in one workspace do not appear in another. # Two kinds of accounts Vidflow distinguishes accounts that can actuall

title: Connect TikTok
description: Link TikTok to Vidflow and understand the settings every TikTok post requires.
body: Connect TikTok to publish and schedule videos to your TikTok account directly from Vidflow. Beta note Beta note: TikTok posts currently publish as private (visible only to you) while the TikTok app review completes. Vidflow marks the post and emails you once a post is affected, and switches to normal publishing automatically when the review finishes. # How it works Vidflow uploads your video file directly to TikTok's Content Posting API, in chunks for larger files, rather than pointing TikTok at a URL. Before every post, Vidflow asks TikTok for your account's current creator settings, live, not cached, so the privacy options and comment/duet/stitch toggles you see always match what your TikTok account actually allows right now. TikTok itself enforces which privacy levels an app is allowed to use: an app still under review is capped at posting privately no matter what you request, and Vidflow surfaces that as a note on the post rather than hiding the difference. # Connect steps Open Workspace Settings . Click the Connections tab. Click TikTok . Sign in on TikTok's page and approve access. You are returned to Vidflow with TikTok listed as connected. # What Vidflow can publish Vidflow publishes video posts to TikTok, both from Post now on library content and from scheduled dispatch. A video file is required, TikTok cannot post text-only or image-only content. # The TikTok settings panel Every TikTok post, whether posted now or scheduled, includes a required settings panel: Posti

title: Connect X
description: Link X (Twitter) to Vidflow to publish posts and know the media size limit.
body: Connect X (Twitter) to publish text and media posts from Vidflow. # How it works Vidflow posts through X's v2 API using an OAuth 2.0 token that carries a rotating refresh token. Vidflow refreshes it automatically in the background well before it expires, and every refresh attempt is deduplicated so overlapping schedule and Post Now activity can't burn through the one-time-use refresh token by racing each other. When media is attached, it's uploaded to X first and the resulting media id is attached to the post; video uploads process on X's side asynchronously, which is why a video post can take a couple of minutes to fully appear. # Connect steps Open Workspace Settings . Click the Connections tab. Click X (Twitter) . Sign in on X's page and approve access. X always shows its login screen here, so you can connect a different or additional account even if one is already signed in on your device. You are returned to Vidflow with X listed as connected. # What Vidflow can publish Text and media posts to your connected X account, from Post now on library content and from scheduled dispatch on both content items and legacy queue posts. # Limits enforced Post text: 280 characters. Scheduled content items are automatically sliced to this length. Media: 512MB hard cap. Video uploads process asynchronously on X's side, which can take up to about 2 minutes before the post finishes. # Token lifetime and reconnect X issues rotating refresh tokens, and Vidflow refreshes them automatically i

title: Connect YouTube
description: Link your YouTube channel to Vidflow to publish episodes and schedule clips.
body: Connect YouTube to upload full episodes and publish scheduled clips to your channel from Vidflow. # What you need before connecting A Google account with a YouTube channel. You will sign in with that Google account and grant Vidflow permission to upload and read your channel. # How it works Uploads use YouTube's resumable upload API: Vidflow determines the file's size, opens an upload session with your title, description, tags, and chosen privacy, then streams the video bytes straight from storage to YouTube. Your Google authorization refreshes automatically in the background a few minutes before it would expire, so day-to-day use rarely needs attention. YouTube's own daily upload quota is tracked separately from anything Vidflow enforces, if a scheduled upload hits it, Vidflow reads that specific error back from Google and defers the post instead of treating it as a failure. # Connect steps Open Workspace Settings . Click the Connections tab. Click YouTube . Sign in with the Google account that owns your channel and approve access. You are returned to Vidflow with YouTube listed as connected. # What Vidflow can publish YouTube works a little differently from the other platforms: Full episodes publish from the episode's own Publishing tab, not from Post now on library content. You choose the privacy for each upload: Public , Private , or Unlisted . Vidflow defaults new uploads to Private so you can review before making them public. Scheduled clips publish through your regular

title: Bulk actions
description: Re-tag, set default accounts, copy between workspaces, or delete many library items in one pass with Bulk Manage.
body: Bulk actions change many library items in one pass instead of opening them one at a time. They run from the Bulk Manage Content dialog. Note Note: Bulk actions are available on plans that include them. See Plans and features . # How it works Every bulk action is the same three-step shape: pick an action tile, select the items it should touch, then apply. The action runs as one batch request, so you get a single confirmation with a count rather than one confirmation per item. Bulk Actions in the sidebar opens the library straight into this dialog. # Open Bulk Manage In the rail, click Content Library , then Bulk Actions . The library opens with the Bulk Manage Content dialog and four actions: Re-Tag Content : add or override tags on the selected items. Select Default Accounts : add or override the default accounts the selected items post to. Delete : delete multiple items at once. Copy Content to Other Workspaces : copy the selected items into another workspace. # Select the content Every action starts with the same picker. Click Select content to manage , then check the items to include. Use the search box to narrow the list, and Select All or Deselect All to move faster. The dialog shows how many items are selected. # Re-tag content Choose Re-Tag Content and select your items. Pick the tags to apply. You can create a new tag inline. Check Remove Existing Content Tags to replace the items' tags instead of adding to them. Apply. A confirmation reports how many items were re-ta

title: Edit a post
description: Open any library item to change its title, caption, platforms, tags, and status, or send it for approval.
body: Every item in the Content Library opens in the same editor. This article walks through editing a text post or image, then summarizes how clips and episodes differ. # How it works Opening an item first shows a read-only detail view, so a click never accidentally starts an edit. Edit Content switches that same modal into an editable form for the title, body, platforms, captions, and tags, backed by one save call. If the item carries a link back to the episode it came from (clips, and text or images generated by Magic Text Posts, Generate Images, or a Flow), caption regeneration reads that episode's transcript as source material instead of only rephrasing the existing caption, which is why linked items tend to caption better. See Get better results below. # Open the editor Open the Content Library and click any item. It opens read-only first. Click Edit Content to make changes. Use the arrows at the top to step to the previous or next item. A counter shows your position in the current list. Click Save when you are done, or Cancel to discard changes. # What you can edit Internal Title : a name for your own reference. It is optional and never published. Caption (image posts) or Post Content (text posts): the text that publishes. A character counter tracks the limit for each platform. Tags : click tag pills to add or remove them. See Tags . Post to : toggle the platforms this item publishes to. Status : Draft , Queued , Scheduled , or Published . See Content Library basics for what

title: Content Library basics
description: Find, filter, and sort everything in your Vidflow library, and understand what the status and approval labels mean.
body: The Content Library holds everything in a workspace: text posts, images, clips, articles, and the episodes they came from. This article shows you how to find things fast and what the status labels mean. Open it from the rail: click Content Library , then All Content . # How it works Every item you upload, generate with AI, or produce through a Flow lands in one shared library scoped to the current workspace. Content carries two independent labels: a status that controls whether and when it publishes, and an approval state that tracks client or internal review. Filters, search, and sort all run against the same underlying list, so any combination narrows down from everything in the workspace. # Tabs Three tabs split the library by what you are looking at: All Content shows every item in the workspace. Episodes shows only your long-form source videos. Clips shows only clips generated from episodes. Episodes are sources, not posts. See Episodes vs. library content for how the two relate. If the library is empty, the page offers shortcuts such as Upload Video , Import from YouTube , and Magic Text Posts to create your first content. # Views and sorting The view button on the left of the filter row switches between Grid View , Compact View , and List View . The sort menu next to it orders items by Date Created , Title A-Z , Unused First , Unapproved First , or Media Type . # Filters The same sort menu also holds the filters. They stack with search: All Types , or one content type:

title: Tags
description: Create color-coded tags to organize your library and route content into schedules, imports, and Flows.
body: Tags organize your content library into custom categories: series, campaigns, content pillars, or anything else that fits your brand. They also do routing work, since schedules, auto-import, and Flows can all act on tags. Tags are available on every plan. # How it works A tag is a name and a color scoped to your workspace. Applying a tag just adds its id to that piece of content, so the same tag can sit on text posts, images, clips, and episodes at once. Nothing else in Vidflow reads the tag's color, only its name and id, so color is purely a visual aid for you and your team. # Create a tag Open the Content Library and click Manage Tags in the filter row. Under New Tag , type a name and pick a color. Ten preset swatches are offered, or click the color square for a custom color. Click Add . # Edit or delete a tag In the same Manage Tags dialog, the Existing Tags list shows every tag in the workspace. Use the controls on a tag's row to rename it, change its color, or delete it. Deleting asks for confirmation and removes the tag from all content that carries it. The content itself is not deleted. # Tag your content One item: open it and click the tag pills in the editor. See Edit a post . Many items: use Re-Tag Content in Bulk Manage. See Bulk actions . Episodes: the episode's Details tab has a Tags field. Separate tags with commas. Automatically: auto-import sources and Flows can tag content for you (see below). # Where tags work Library filters : narrow the library to one tag

title: Build your first flow
description: Create a flow in the editor, connect nodes and router branches, add branch filters, then save and run it.
body: Build a flow that turns each new episode into content automatically. This walkthrough covers the editor, connecting nodes, router branches, branch filters, and the first run. Note Note: Building and running a flow you create yourself requires the Pro or Agency plan. Every workspace already has one flow running for free: First Content Pack , installed automatically and active from day one. See Flows overview and Plans and features . # How it works A flow is a graph of nodes saved as one record: a trigger, the processing and publishing nodes you connect to it, and the edges between them. Running a flow walks that graph starting from the trigger, executes each node in turn (an AI node calls the model, a Save node writes to the library, and so on), and writes a step-by-step log you can read afterward in History . Nothing is committed to your library until a run reaches a Save or Post node, so you can safely build and test a flow before it ever creates real content. # Create the flow Open Flows in the sidebar. Click New Flow , or click Use Template on a template (including First Content Pack , if you want to start from the flow every workspace already has) to start from a prebuilt pipeline. In the left panel, enter a Workflow Name and an optional Description . In the toolbar, pick the trigger: Episode Upload , Google Drive File , or Manual Trigger . # Get to know the editor The left panel holds the node palette, grouped into Triggers, Processing, Publishing, Logic, and Reviews. Th

title: Flow nodes reference
description: Every node in the flow editor palette with its options, grouped the way the palette groups them.
body: Every node you can place in a flow, with its options. Nodes are listed in the same groups as the editor palette: Triggers, Processing, Publishing, Logic, and Reviews. # How it works Each node is a small, self-contained step: a trigger starts a run, a processing node calls the AI or clip engine and produces one or more in-memory drafts, a logic node changes what happens next, and a publishing node is what actually commits a draft to your library or a queue. A run walks the graph from its trigger, executing each connected node once and passing its drafts downstream to whatever it feeds into. The same node type behaves identically whether you place it in a flow you built yourself or find it inside the First Content Pack template every workspace starts with. Two things apply to every node: Each node has a Label field in its configuration panel so you can rename it on the canvas. Generated content travels down the flow as drafts. Enrichment nodes (captions, images, tags) modify those drafts, but nothing is stored until the drafts reach a Save or Post node. # Triggers A flow has one trigger, chosen in the toolbar. The trigger nodes in the palette match the same three options. Episode Upload Starts the flow whenever a new episode is created in this workspace. No configuration. The flow's Active switch must be on for it to fire, and each run waits for the episode's transcript before starting; see Runs, history, and troubleshooting . Google Drive File Starts the flow from a file dropp

title: Runs, history, and troubleshooting
description: How flow runs start, why episode-upload runs wait for the transcript, and how to read the run history.
body: Every execution of a flow is a run. This article covers how runs start, the waiting state on episode uploads, the history log, and the most common failures. # How it works A run is a single pass through a flow's saved graph, tracked as its own record with a status and a step-by-step log. The engine starts at the trigger, executes each connected node once, and follows the outgoing edges whose branch filters pass, so a run with a router can fan out into more than one path in the same execution. The log it produces is what powers both the History panel below and the live progress screen you see the first time you add an episode. # How runs start Automatically. An Episode Upload flow with its Active switch on queues a run whenever a new episode is created in the workspace. Every new workspace starts with one such flow already active: First Content Pack . Its run is what you watch build on the onboarding screen the first time you add an episode. Manually from the Flows page. Click Run on the flow card, pick an episode (uses its transcript) or a saved script (uses the script text), then click Run flow . Manually from the editor. Enter an episode's ID in Episode ID for Run and click Run . Without an ID the run starts with no source, and steps that need one are skipped. A flow must be saved before it can run. In a brand-new flow, Run stays disabled until the first Save . # The waiting state Episode-upload runs do not start the moment the episode is created. The transcript is still be

title: Flows overview
description: What Flows automate, which plans include them, and how triggers, templates, and runs fit together.
body: Flows are Vidflow's automation builder. You draw a pipeline once, and every new episode comes out the other side as clips, text posts, and images, saved to your library and queued for publishing. # What a flow is A flow is a chain of nodes on a canvas. It starts with a trigger, runs your content through processing nodes (clips, text posts, blog posts, captions, images, tags), and ends in a publishing node that saves the results. Until a Save node runs, nothing generated by the flow is stored, so every flow should end in Save to Library , Save to Queue , or another publishing node. Flows belong to a workspace. Open Flows in the sidebar to see the flows for the workspace you are in. Note Note: Building, editing, and manually running a flow in the Flows editor requires the Pro or Agency plan. The requirement is enforced when you create a flow and when you run one. See Plans and features . The one exception is the First Content Pack below, which runs automatically on every plan. # Your first flow runs itself Every new workspace gets a flow named First Content Pack installed automatically, no setup required. It is active by default and fires the moment you add your first episode: it generates a handful of clips, a few text posts for Instagram, X, and LinkedIn, and a small set of quote-card images, writes per-platform captions on all of it, tags everything "First pack," and drops the results into your default publishing queue. This runs on every plan, including Free, because it is

title: The dashboard at a glance
description: What each card on the Dashboard shows, from stat cards and upcoming posts to the getting-started checklist and tips.
body: The Dashboard is what you land on when you open a workspace's Home . It is a single-page overview of where your content stands: what you've made, what's about to publish, what needs your attention, and a couple of nudges to help you finish setting up. # How it works Every card on the Dashboard reads live data scoped to your current workspace, there's nothing to configure. Switching workspaces refreshes every card to that workspace's own numbers. If no workspace is selected yet, the Dashboard shows a No Workspace Selected message with a shortcut to create one instead of any of the cards below. # What's on the Dashboard Stat cards. Four numbers up top: Total Episodes, Total Content, Pending Approval, and Scheduled Posts. Each one links straight to the relevant screen (episodes in your library, the full library, Approvals, or the Calendar). A small alert icon appears on Pending Approval when it's above zero. Getting started checklist. A dismissible card that tracks six setup steps, brand voice, connecting an account, creating content, building a schedule, scheduling a post, and setting up approvals, with a progress bar and a Replay setup guide link. It disappears on its own once every step is complete, or you can dismiss it early with the X. Upcoming Scheduled Posts. The next several posts headed to your calendar, with their platforms and how soon they go out. Click any row, or View Calendar , to jump to the full Calendar. Unread Comments. Client and teammate comments left on ap

title: Your first content pack
description: What happens after onboarding when you add your first episode, and how Vidflow turns it into a ready-to-schedule batch of content automatically.
headings: What you get depends on how you added it
body: Right after you finish or skip the onboarding wizard, Vidflow asks for one thing: your first episode. Hand it a YouTube link or a video file and it builds a full batch of content from it automatically, no need to open the Clip Generator or Magic Text Posts yourself for this first run. # How it works Submitting your first episode kicks off Vidflow's default content flow against it: the episode is transcribed, then the AI writes text posts, designs images, and writes captions, all in the brand voice and image style you set up during onboarding, tags everything, and queues it to your calendar. If you uploaded a video file, native clip generation runs too, cutting short clips straight from the footage. You do not have to trigger any of this by hand, it starts the moment the episode is submitted and you can watch it happen on a live progress screen. # Add your first episode On the Add your first episode screen, choose Paste a YouTube link or Upload a file . For a YouTube link, paste the URL and click Add . For a file, click Choose video or audio file and pick a video or audio file from your computer. Vidflow starts working immediately and switches to the progress screen. You can also click Skip for now below the card to go straight to your Dashboard and add an episode later from the Make Content menu instead. # What you get depends on how you added it This is the one honest tradeoff worth knowing up front: Uploaded file : the full pack, posts, images, captions, and native clips cu

title: Getting access to Vidflow
description: Vidflow is invite-only during the beta. Here is how the waitlist and invite process works.
headings: If you try to sign in without an invite
body: Vidflow is currently invite-only, with new accounts onboarded in waves from the waitlist. # How it works Every sign-in, Google or email and password, is checked against an invite list before it's allowed through. There is no open signup path around that check. Joining the waitlist just records your email; it doesn't grant access on its own. Access is granted in waves as the Vidflow team decides they have capacity, and an invite email is what actually unlocks sign-in for that address. # How access works today There is no open signup. Every sign-in is checked against an invite list, whether you sign in with Google or with email and password. If your email is not on that list, you land on the waitlist page instead of the app. Beta note Beta note: Vidflow is in an invite-only beta. Access opens in waves as capacity allows. # If you try to sign in without an invite If you attempt to sign in with an email that has not been invited, you are redirected to a waitlist page. Depending on your situation, you will see one of two messages: "Vidflow is invite only." if your email has never been seen before. "Your account has not been approved yet." if your email was already caught by the process once. Either way, the page lets you join the waitlist right there. Submitting the form always confirms your spot, whether or not your email was already known to Vidflow. # After you join the waitlist There is no published timeline for when waitlisted accounts get invited. Access is granted in waves,

title: Finding your way around
description: A tour of the rail, the Make Content button, notifications, and where to get help inside Vidflow.
body: Once you are inside a workspace, everything in Vidflow is reachable from a left-hand navigation rail, a global Make Content button, and a notifications bell. # How it works The rail, the Make Content button, and the notifications bell stay the same on every page, they're the app's fixed chrome. Everything else, the page you're actually looking at, changes underneath them. Learning this shell once means every other article in the help center can just tell you which button to click, without re-explaining where the app's frame is. # The navigation rail The rail has seven sections: Home : opens your Dashboard, with your upcoming schedule, quick actions, and recent activity. Content Library : All Content, Bulk Actions, and Auto-Import. Publish : Calendar and Schedules under Schedule, Approvals under Review. Magic Tools : Clip Generator, Idea Generator, Idea Library, Script Generator, and Script Library under Create; Magic Text Posts and Blog Post under Distribute. Flows : opens the Flows automation builder directly. Analytics : Overview and Top Content. Settings : opens Workspace Settings.
